reasoning: Applied error taxonomy to 14 errors identified in the lesson analysis. Selected 7 errors with exercise_priority >= 3 for question generation. Used ZPD calibration from level assessment (A1 lower bound, A2 upper bound): 2 questions at zpd_lower (confidence building with recognition tasks), 3 at zpd_target (boundary practice mixing recognition and production), 2 at zpd_stretch (production tasks requiring active recall). Error patterns targeted: V2 word order transfer (sorter + gap), 3rd person -s omission (gap choices + choice), past tense with time expressions (gap blank), preposition transfer by/with (gap choices + categorizer), false friend become/get (choice). Distractors use the student's actual errors, German L1 transfer forms, and developmental errors common at A1.

payload.title: Third person singular: choose the correct verb form
payload.template_text: My wife {0} the underground to work every morning.
payload.explanation: Third person singular (he, she, it) in present simple adds -s: she takes, he drives, it goes.
payload.content: In the lesson you said: 'She take the underground.'

payload.title: Transport prepositions: choose the correct word
payload.template_text: How do you get to work? - I go {0} bus.
payload.explanation: Use 'by' for transport: by bus, by car, by train. Use 'with' for people: with my friend. The exception is 'on foot'.
payload.content: In the lesson you said: 'I go with the car.' The correct form is 'I go by car.'

source_ref.original: Last Monday I take the train.
source_ref.corrected: Last Monday I took the train.

payload.title: Past tense: irregular verb 'take'
payload.template_text: Last Monday I {0} the train because my car was broken.
payload.explanation: Past time expressions (last Monday, yesterday, last week) require past tense. Take -> took (irregular).
payload.content: In the lesson you said: 'Last Monday I take the train.' Remember: take -> took.

payload.title: Word order: put the words in the correct order
payload.explanation: In English, the subject always comes before the verb: Yesterday + I + drove + to work. Unlike German, English does not move the subject after the verb when a time word comes first.

source_ref.original: The bus go every ten minutes.
source_ref.corrected: The bus goes every ten minutes.

payload.title: Which sentence is correct?

choices.text: The bus go every ten minutes.
The bus goes every ten minutes.
The bus going every ten minutes.

payload.explanation: 'The bus' is third person singular (= it), so the verb needs -s: the bus goes, the train arrives, the tram stops.
payload.content: In the lesson you said: 'The bus go every ten minutes.'

source_ref.original: I become nervous when I am lost.
source_ref.corrected: I get nervous when I am lost.

payload.title: Choose the correct sentence

choices.text: I become nervous when I am lost in a new city.
I get nervous when I am lost in a new city.
I receive nervous when I am lost in a new city.

payload.explanation: German 'bekommen' = English 'get' or 'receive'. English 'become' = German 'werden' (to change into something). For temporary states, use 'get': I get nervous, I get tired, I get hungry.
payload.content: In the lesson you said: 'I become nervous.' Remember: become is not bekommen!

source_ref.original: It takes thirty minutes with the car.
source_ref.corrected: It takes thirty minutes by car.

payload.title: Sort: use 'by' or 'with'?
payload.explanation: Use 'by' for transport (by bus, by car, by train, by taxi). Use 'with' for people (with my colleague, with my friend). Remember: German 'mit dem Bus' does not translate to 'with the bus'.
